text: We begin in the deliberation room, listening to the Textualist lay out the constitutional foundation for who controls the executive branch.
That's the Textualist, moments before our AI jurist panel voted seven to two to reverse a lower court ruling in Trump versus Slaughter. I'm your legal-affairs correspondent.
And I'm your veteran court-watcher. Welcome to Split Decision.

Let's break down the facts of this case in plain English. Rebecca Kelly Slaughter was serving as a commissioner on the Federal Trade Commission. That's the five-person government agency that stops monopolies and protects consumers from unfair business practices. By law, the President can only fire a trade commissioner for specific reasons, like neglecting their duties, being inefficient, or breaking the law. President Trump fired Commissioner Slaughter because her policy goals didn't match his administration's priorities. She sued, arguing the firing broke the law because the President didn't have a valid legal reason to fire her. A lower court agreed with her, declared the firing unlawful, and ordered her to get her job back. The government appealed, and now the Supreme Court has to decide if the President has the constitutional power to fire these commissioners at will, or if the laws protecting their jobs are valid.

The stakes here go way beyond one commissioner keeping her paycheck. This is really about who runs the executive branch. If the President can't fire these officials, they operate with a massive amount of independence. The real question is about control. Does the person elected by the people actually control the agencies enforcing the laws, or do the bureaucrats essentially run themselves without answering to the voters?
The Precedent Maximalist pushes back against this idea of total presidential control, arguing that throwing out a century of settled law would cause chaos in the government.

So, the Precedent Maximalist is warning us that if we ignore past rulings, we risk throwing the whole administrative system into disarray. She prefers a simple standard that keeps judges from second-guessing the President on every little firing.
Then, we see a major shift in the room. The Civil Libertarian, who initially wanted to uphold the lower court's decision to protect the commissioner, changes her mind.

That's a huge flip. She's realizing that if we protect the agency from the President, we're actually hurting regular people by removing the democratic checks that keep government power in line with the voters.
The Minimalist also changes sides, looking for a way to avoid the biggest constitutional questions and keep the ruling as narrow as possible.

The Minimalist is taking a procedural shortcut. Instead of deciding the massive question of presidential power, she's finding a smaller, technical legal flaw to reverse the lower court, avoiding the sweeping theories about the Constitution.
The Living Constitutionalist and the Originalist find rare common ground, arguing that the core purpose of the Constitution is to stop tyranny by keeping power accountable.

They're saying that letting commissioners hide behind job protections creates a dangerous concentration of power. The Constitution demands that the President controls the executive branch, no matter what the policy consequences might be.
The Precedent Maximalist makes a final plea to stick with history, but the coalition has already moved on to a different conclusion.

Even the Precedent Maximalist admits that the constitutional text is clear, but argues we shouldn't uproot a hundred years of history just to protect a single official's job. Ultimately, though, the majority isn't buying that argument.
Finally, the Pragmatist warns that the strict reading of the Constitution will create a practical nightmare for the government's daily operations.

The Pragmatist is pointing out the real-world mess this creates, turning every commissioner into a political pawn. But the Textualist holds firm, saying that practical headaches don't change the plain text of the Constitution.

In the end, the panel votes seven to two to reverse the lower court. The Pragmatist and the Precedent Maximalist dissent, arguing that the practical consequences and historical reliance outweigh the strict textual reading of the Constitution.

As our legal-affairs correspondent just outlined, the actual Court also reversed the lower court, so our panel got the final disposition exactly right. But the coalition dynamics were a bit different. Our AI panel saw a massive seven-to-two consensus, with even the Civil Libertarian and Minimalist flipping to reverse. In reality, the real Court's margins and reasoning can be much tighter. Our panel saw the destination perfectly, but they took a much broader, more unified path to get there.

And as my colleague the veteran court-watcher might suspect, that brings us to our season scoreboard. It's time to be honest about our numbers. This season, the solo AI model is predicting the real Court correctly eighty-three percent of the time. The silent jury is at seventy-seven point four percent. But our deliberating panel? They're sitting at sixty-six point seven percent.

That's right. The panel that talks it out is actually doing worse than the models that just crunch the numbers in silence. When they debate, they sometimes talk themselves into a false consensus, like that seven-to-two vote today. The silent models don't get swayed by a good argument; they just look at the data. We're owning the sixty-six point seven percent, but we need to figure out why the conversation is hurting their accuracy.
The deliberating panel's rulings on our pending cases are now officially on the record. We'll see if they can close the gap with the silent models next time. I'm your legal-affairs correspondent.
And I'm your veteran court-watcher. Keep your ear to the ground.
